data.title: Judge Scraps Trump's $15 Billion New York Times Defamation Lawsuit — WSJ
data.description:
By Louise Radnofsky

A federal judge on Friday struck President Trump's $15 billion defamation lawsuit against the New York Times, characterizing the president's legal complaint as a vitriolic screed that violated basic court requirements for suing someone.

U.S. Judge Steven Merryday's order striking the complaint came just four days after it was filed by the president in the Middle District of Florida. He gave the president's lawyers 28 days to file a new version.

"As every lawyer knows (or is presumed to know), a complaint is not a public forum for vituperation and invective — not a protected platform to rage against an adversary," Merryday wrote. "A complaint is not a megaphone for public relations or a podium for a passionate oration at a political rally or the functional equivalent of the Hyde Park Speakers' Corner."

Trump's lawsuit had named as defendants the Times and four of its reporters, alongside book publisher Penguin Random House. It accused the paper of interference in the 2024 presidential election and of "spreading false and defamatory content" about Trump.

The complaint cited several Times articles and singled out a book written by reporters of the newspaper and published by Penguin Random House titled "Lucky Loser: How Donald Trump Squandered His Father's Fortune and Created the Illusion of Success."

The judge, who was appointed to the bench by George H.W. Bush, called the complaint "decidedly improper and impermissible."

Merryday noted that while the lawsuit alleged two straightforward counts of defamation, it "consumes eighty-five pages. Count I appears on page eighty, and Count II appears on page eighty-three," he said. The complaint, he added, contains "many, often repetitive, and laudatory (toward President Trump) but superfluous allegations."

A lawyer for Trump who signed the complaint didn't immediately respond to a request for comment, nor did the New York Times or Penguin Random House. The Times had previously said the suit "lacks any legitimate legal claims and instead is an attempt to stifle and discourage independent reporting."

Trump has filed a separate lawsuit against the publisher of The Wall Street Journal in the Southern District of Florida, alleging the newspaper defamed him in an article about a birthday letter sent to disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein. A Dow Jones spokeswoman has said of that case, "We have full confidence in the rigor and accuracy of our reporting, and will vigorously defend against any lawsuit."

Merryday said that the president's legal team can file an amended complaint within 28 days, but cautioned: "This action will begin, will continue, and will end in accord with the rules of procedure and in a professional and dignified manner."

The amended complaint, he concluded, must not exceed 40 pages, "excluding only the caption, the signature, and any attachment."

Write to Louise Radnofsky at louise.radnofsky@wsj.com

data.title: The Stock Market Got a Rate-Cut Boost. Where It's Headed Next. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Teresa Rivas

This week was one where everything went right. Expect the good times to continue for at least the near future.

The S&P 500 was gaining 0.3%, as was the Dow Jones Industrial Average, while the Nasdaq Composite was advancing 0.4%. All three were on track to close the week at new highs after Thursday's records — the fifth for the Dow, the 26th for the S&P, and the 27th for the Nasdaq — and small-caps joined the party too, with the Russell 2000 hitting its first record of 2025.

Celebrations were in order. The Federal Reserve finally cut interest rates, and while the decision to lower them by a quarter-point wasn't a surprise, it hit the mark. No surprise half-point cut emerged to spark concern, and while some were hoping for more consensus about future cuts, Fed Chair Jerome Powell "expressed confidence in the current state of the U.S. economy and financial conditions," notes DataTrek Research co-founder Nicholas Colas. "The Chair's comments here were his most bullish but also his shortest."

To wit, Powell wouldn't opine on whether a rate cut would further fuel a speculative stock bubble — maybe a good thing given how Alan Greenspan's "irrational exuberance" comments turned out — but the composition of the rally is reassuring. As SentimenTrader highlights, the S&P 500's new record came alongside new simultaneous peaks for its key sectors, as the Technology Select Sector SPDR, Communication Services Select Sector SPDR, and Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR exchange-traded funds also hit new highs. This kind of broad-based rally is a healthy one for markets, "where growth-oriented sectors take the lead as the 'vanguard,' with other economically sensitive sectors (such as Financials and Industrials) following closely," the firm wrote.

S&P 500 breakthroughs tend to be narrow — often, they're led by only one or two sectors hitting new highs simultaneously — so this week's trifecta represents a "significant upgrade in market participation-a typical feature of historically healthy, broad-based bull market rallies, " SentimenTrader writes. In the 86 times when that has happened previously, the market tends to be higher a year later nearly three-quarters of the time, with a median return of more than 8%, "demonstrating a certain degree of robustness as a long-term trend indicator."

We don't want to pretend there aren't concerns. The job market is still weakening. President Donald Trump is still tariffing. Stocks are still expensive. Powell himself said that risk-free paths don't exist, warning that policymakers are still walking a tightrope.

Even yields on 10-year Treasuries have moved higher, snapping a four-week streak of falling, as investors reposition after the cut and those who had hoped for an even more dovish tone.

The latter, though, shouldn't worry investors for now, says 22V Research's Dennis DeBusschere. He notes that the 10-year yield and stocks should move in the same direction as recession risks fade, at least until the 10-year gets back to 4.5%, the level that has caused problems in the recent past. But with yields at just 4.14%, that is a problem for the future.

For now, let the party continue.

Write to Teresa Rivas at teresa.rivas@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: DuPont de Nemours Is Maintained at Outperform by BMO Capital
Porsche Cuts Guidance Again After EV Market Woes Prompt Lineup Review — Update
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo and Mauro Orru

Porsche AG cut its guidance for the year again as it continues to reel from a slow electric-vehicle market, fierce competition from Chinese rivals and President Trump's tariffs.

The German premium-car maker said it had been forced to conduct a significant review of its product portfolio, delaying the launch of some new fully electric vehicle models and adding models with combustion and hybrid drivetrains to supplement its lineup.

A new SUV Series won't initially be available as all-electric vehicles as originally planned. Instead, those vehicles will be offered exclusively as combustion engines and plug-in hybrids. Current models such as the Panamera and the Cayenne will also be available with combustion engines and as plug-in hybrids well into the 2030s.

The group expects to spend around 3.1 billion euros ($2.12 billion) on its realignment. Of this, up to 1.8 billion euros will go toward the rescheduling of a new platform for electric vehicles. As a result, Porsche now expects a return on sales of up to 2% this year, down from a previous forecast range of 5% to 7%.

It also lowered its automotive earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization margin guidance to between 10.5% and 12.5%, from a previous range of 14.5% to 16.5%. It continues to expect sales between 37 billion euros and 38 billion euros.

Porsche has been grappling with a slow EV market. Last year, it scaled back its EV ambitions and vowed to expand its portfolio in the coming years to include models with combustion engines and plug-in hybrid powertrains.

Earlier this year, Porsche unveiled plans to shed around 3,900 jobs by 2029 in a bid to become more efficient and inject new life into the brand.

Aside from EV market woes and declining demand in China, Porsche is also reckoning with 27.5% import tariffs in the U.S. President Trump plans to keep those duties on EU auto imports until the bloc introduces legislation to reduce levies on a variety of U.S. goods.

The company slashed guidance in July and April as it warned investors tariffs would have a negative effect. Its decision to cut its outlook for the second time in less than two months on Friday had ripple effects on Volkswagen and Porsche SE, the entity controlled by the Porsche-Piech family that holds big stakes in both companies.

Volkswagen, which owns more than 75% of Porsche AG, said its operating profit this year would take a hit of around 5.1 billion euros because of Porsche's forecast adjustments and portfolio review. Volkswagen now expects an operating return on sales to between 2% and 3% this year, compared with 4% to 5% previously.

Meanwhile, Porsche SE--which has a direct equity investment of around 12.5% in Porsche AG--is expecting an adjusted after tax profit of 900 million euros to 2.9 billion euros this year, below previous guidance of 1.6 billion euros to 3.6 billion euros.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com and Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1353 ET - Royal Bank of Canada expects data to show the Canadian economy rebounded with vigor in July following a dismal 2Q. The bank's economists tell clients they are penciling in a 0.2% month-over-month increase in industry-level GDP, when Statistics Canada issues the monthly data next week. The data agency has forecast a 0.1% jump. RBC points to solid volume gains in exports, manufacturing shipments and wholesale transactions, and an anticipated pickup in oil production in western Canada after forest fires forced companies to curtail production. Employment fell sharply in July, but RBC notes that the drop in hours worked was not as steep. Overall, RBC expects "modest" GDP growth in 3Q after a 1.6% annualized drop in 2Q. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1332 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose by two this week to 418 in a fourth straight increase, and was 70 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. With a potential drop in oil prices below $60 this winter on oversupply concerns, some shale producers might struggle to break even "and could be forced to shut in significant volumes of production, potentially rebalancing the market and pushing prices back into the $60s," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "Rig counts in the U.S. have been steadily declining since 2023, with an accelerated drop since early this year, indicating that producers are already scaling back growth and may soon begin curtailing output." Natural gas rigs are steady at 118, up by 22 from a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1046 ET - Canadian electricity flows into the Northeastern U.S. have been declining in recent years, partly because of drought limiting Canada's hydroelectricity generation and partly because of lower demand in the New York and New England independent system operators, the EIA says in a note. Canadian electricity imports provided 11% of NY-ISO electricity between 2016 and 2022. That fell to 3% in 2024 and 2% through August of this year. Canadian imports play a key role at times of high demand. In January Canada supplied 14% of NE-ISO's power, although on average through August it provided 5% compared with 14% between 2016 and 2022. While typically importers, "shifting trends have led to multiple days of net electricity exports into Canada from the two ISOs," the EIA adds. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1016 ET - European natural-gas prices continue to trade in a tight range as traders weigh a fresh package of EU sanctions against Russia and Norwegian gas outages. In afternoon trade, the benchmark Dutch TTF contract is down 1.5% to 32.47 euros a megawatt hour and on track for a modest weekly loss. The new package of sanctions includes a ban on Russian liquefied natural gas imports, with EU representative Kaja Kallas saying on X that the aim is to speed up the phaseout by Jan. 1, 2027. "Russia still covers more than 10% of EU's gas imports, roughly half of which comes via LNG," ANZ analysts say. "With global LNG markets expected to push into a surplus later this decade, any sanctions in the short-term will have an impact on prices." Meanwhile, investors are monitoring unplanned gas field outages in Norway, Europe's single largest supplier of gas. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1007 ET - Citi analysts keep a "less-bearish-than-consensus" view of oil prices, seeing Brent easing toward $60 a barrel by the end of this year and into 2026. The OPEC+ decision to start unwinding a second layer of output cuts adds to expected global stock builds, which Citi now pegs at 1.1 million barrels a day in 2025 and 2.1 million b/d in 2026. The bank's bear case that could push Brent toward $50 could come about if global demand erodes on weaker macroeconomics and trade, supply rises faster than expected, "and if China stockpiling disappoints," they say in their quarterly outlook. The low-probability bull case with Brent above $75 would be driven by geopolitical events such as attacks on Russian infrastructure or an Israel-Iran escalation. Brent is off 0.7% at $66.97 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0917 ET - U.S. natural gas futures add to yesterday's losses following a bigger-than-expected inventory build that put storage levels 204 billion cubic feet above the five-year average. "The key near-term question is if technical support that tested as low as $2.869 on Sunday evening can hold," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. "Amid renewed risks of the storage trajectory overshooting higher, October weather may be the decisive factor." Nymex natural gas is off 2.3% at $2.872/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0915 ET - Oil futures are lower as the EU unveils a new package of sanctions against Russia that targets banks, oil vessels and buyers of Russian oil, while lowering the crude oil price cap. Robert Yawger of Mizuho said this week that the previous 18 rounds of EU sanctions since the 2022 invasion of Ukraine "have proven largely ineffective in stopping the flow of Russian oil exports." Ukrainian attacks on Russian oil facilities lifted prices early this week. WTI is down 0.5% at $63.26 a barrel and Brent is off 0.4% at $67.20. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0607 ET - China Railway Group is expected to see a recovery in growth for new orders for FY 2025, says OCBC's equity research team, pointing to the company's target of CNY2.8T for the period. OCBC says the issuance of special bonds in 2H25 and likely improved order execution are expected to support the recovery. The company also expects margins to improve through cost controls and lower financing costs. OCBC holds a buy rating on China Railway Group's stock, setting its fair value estimate at HK$5.39, with its H shares last closing at HK$3.76. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0543 ET - Kuehne + Nagel could face challenges in the second half, and Deutsche Bank forecasts a step down in profits in the third quarter, both on year and on quarter. "We see few short-term catalysts, with earnings momentum still likely to be under pressure, volatile tariff news flow, and the risk of a recession which we think is not baked into consensus forecasts," analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank's new 2025 EBIT forecast of 1.35 billion Swiss francs is now below the low-end of recently revised full-year guidance of 1.45 billion-1.65 billion francs. The bank downgrades the shipping and transport company's stock rating to hold from buy and lowers its target price to 182 francs from 225 francs. Shares fall 7.1% to 157.40 francs. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0513 ET - Shares in container shipping companies are under pressure Friday as container freight rates on major routes out of Shanghai continue to fall. The Shanghai Containerized Freight Index has fallen over 14% in the last week, according to data published by the Shanghai Shipping Exchange. "Far East to U.S. west coast rates are now down 56% from its recent peak reached in week 23 and 61% down from week 1," analysts at JPMorgan said. Maersk shares are 5% lower, while Hapag-Lloyd shares trade 2.6% lower. Maritime research consultancy Drewry said in a note that its world container index--that tracks freight rates for 40-foot containers on eight major shipping routes--shows the major Transpacific and Asia-Europe routes are now aligned in a downwards trajectory. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0400 ET - Oil prices are on track for a modest weekly gain as a firmer U.S. dollar weighs on sentiment and markets await more clarity on Western sanctions against Russia. In early trade, Brent crude is down 0.2% to $67.28 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.4% to $62.99 a barrel. Both benchmarks are up only 0.5% this week. "Despite modest weekly gains, crude remains stuck in a narrow range since early August, weighed down by bearish fundamentals including OPEC+'s accelerated output return," says Soojin Kim from MUFG. However, concerns persist over potential disruptions to Russian supplies following recent Ukrainian attacks on key energy infrastructure and President Trump's call for NATO allies to halt purchases of Russian oil. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0401 ET - London-listed miners gain in early morning European trading as gold ticks higher. Gold futures are up 0.2% at $3,685.20 a troy ounce and nearly 10% higher this month on U.S. interest rate cut hopes and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop. Endeavour Mining and Fresnillo are leading the FTSE 100 index risers, up 3% and 2.5%, respectively. Anglo American shares are up 1.3%, while Antofagasta shares are trading 1% higher (aimee.look@wsj.com)

data.title: Darden Restaurants Is Maintained at Market Perform by BMO Capital
data.description: (17:53 GMT) Darden Restaurants Price Target Cut to $205.00/Share From $215.00 by BMO Capital

data.title: Canada to Report Healthy Rebound in July — Market Talk
data.description:
1353 ET - Royal Bank of Canada expects data to show the Canadian economy rebounded with vigor in July following a dismal 2Q. The bank's economists tell clients they are penciling in a 0.2% month-over-month increase in industry-level GDP, when Statistics Canada issues the monthly data next week. The data agency has forecast a 0.1% jump. RBC points to solid volume gains in exports, manufacturing shipments and wholesale transactions, and an anticipated pickup in oil production in western Canada after forest fires forced companies to curtail production. Employment fell sharply in July, but RBC notes that the drop in hours worked was not as steep. Overall, RBC expects "modest" GDP growth in 3Q after a 1.6% annualized drop in 2Q. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1349 ET - The sluggish housing market and affordability concerns from buyers have forced Lennar to build homes more cheaply, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says, adding that the whole industry will need to continue that push. "We're going to have to find, frankly, as an industry, a way to build and deliver homes at a more affordable level," Miller says, mentioning potential cost reductions in land financing and construction. He also says Lennar is building new technologies to cut costs that are expensive to do. "The estimate goes up before it goes down," he says. "We have to get more efficient and effective." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1347 ET - Mortgage rates are steadily declining, falling this week to their lowest level since 2024. But that yet hasn't led to a boost in sales for Lennar, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "Even though mortgage rates began to trend downward towards the end of the quarter, stronger sales have not yet followed," Miller says. But the company is starting to see stronger customer interest and traffic and expects sales to eventually pick up if mortgage rates continue to fall. "We are optimistic that if mortgage rates approach the 6% level or even lower, we will soon see some firming in the market," Miller says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1332 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose by two this week to 418 in a fourth straight increase, and was 70 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. With a potential drop in oil prices below $60 this winter on oversupply concerns, some shale producers might struggle to break even "and could be forced to shut in significant volumes of production, potentially rebalancing the market and pushing prices back into the $60s," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "Rig counts in the U.S. have been steadily declining since 2023, with an accelerated drop since early this year, indicating that producers are already scaling back growth and may soon begin curtailing output." Natural gas rigs are steady at 118, up by 22 from a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1310 ET - Expedia executives see artificial intelligence as a means to reinvent the company again, following previous transformations with a shift to mobile and the rise of the internet, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending an event with CEO Ariane Gorin. The online travel agency benefits from a swath of data and has started to incorporate AI across its platform to summarize guest reviews and answer questions about properties, the analysts say. Those on-platform tools have already led to a clear improvement in conversion, though it's still too early to assess the impact from the company's AI agents and other external AI tools, the analysts say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1302 ET - The housing market is stuck in a vicious cycle in which low housing supply leads to high prices, which discourages home sales, which then leads builders to pull back on increasing supply, Lennar's Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "New construction has slowed as builders have pulled back on production due to slow sales and affordability concerns, therefore exacerbating the chronic supply shortage," Miller says, adding that demand is still high with people wanting and needing homes. "This has been a difficult cycle, as low supply fuels high prices and high prices lock out many of our buyers." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Bill Holdings is due for a bull run as the company tries to outperform low Wall Street growth forecasts, Truist analysts say in a research note as they upgrade shares to buy from hold. The business-assistance software company is set up to exceed analyst expectations in core revenue growth with both easier comps ahead and better-than-expected customer growth in its Embedded Solutions segment, the analysts say. Bill stock has been a laggard in the fintech sector for the past couple of years due to both a lack of investor interest in small- to mid-cap fintech companies and a slowdown in core revenue growth, but that has reset the stock low enough for investors to become bullish again, the analysts say. They also see sizable long positions held by activist shareholders as a plus. Shares rise 0.6% to $52.90. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1234 ET - Bond markets show little reaction to Fed Governor Miran's first public remarks about his dissent at the FOMC meeting, with the 10-year yield hovering around 4.14%. Miran said on CNBC he doubts tariffs will keep inflation hot, while a substantial decline in immigration could cool off housing prices. The Fed cut rates by 25 basis points this week, while Miran proposed a 50-bp trim. "I was sworn in...just about an hour before the meeting started," he said. "I'll be making my arguments in coming weeks and months and I hope that I'll be able to persuade some of my colleagues." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1218 ET - Canada intends to release Friday its own notice kickstarting consultations with businesses, workers and other levels of government regarding the U.S. review of USMCA, the formal trade treaty incorporating the big three North American economies. The U.S. kicked off the USMCA review process this week, ahead of the formal review of the trade treaty on July 1. Analysts expect the review to turn into another round of high-stakes negotiations, with the Trump administration likely pushing for more concessions from Mexico and Canada. Dominic LeBlanc, the Canadian minister in charge of U.S. trade relations, says the government seeks input on how to approach the USMCA review from those "affected by the recent turbulence in the trading relationship," and what specifically Canada should pursue in possible negotiations. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1153 ET - BlackBerry should be able to achieve or beat its guidance in its fiscal 2Q, CIBC's Todd Coupland says in a report. He says BlackBerry's average surprise compared with FactSet is 7% for revenue and almost triple for adjusted EBITDA since John Giamatteo took the helm as CEO at the end of 2023. "We expect this encouraging trend to continue," he says, noting thatshow progress in secure communications, its secure messaging and cybersecurity platform for governments and enterprises, as well as QNX, which is an embedded operating system that is used in automotive, medical and industrial applications. "We see these trends accelerating throughout F2026," Coupland adds. BlackBerry reports its 2Q on Sept. 25. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1148 ET - Greater adoption of artificial intelligence tools is the leading trend in travel, with the number of consumers who used an AI-based app to help with trip planning more than doubling year-over-year to about 30% in 2025, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending a panel with industry executives. While current experimentation of agentic AI in the travel industry is widespread, most companies are using the same undifferentiated AI chatbots, which have yet to materialize into any economic benefit, the analysts say. Companies should be able to monetize AI in the future with vertical use cases, like automating procurement or reimagining call centers, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1116 ET - Cracker Barrel abandoned its logo change and restaurant redesign, but other elements of the company's transformation plan will be leaned into more heavily, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says in a research note. Those elements have been working and can drive both increased traffic and customer frequency as well as stronger profitability, the analyst says. Management has already teased that it will double down on its menu innovation efforts, which have proven successful for the brand, Brooks says. The company will also continue to work on back-of-house efficiency efforts and drive further impact from the brand's 9 million-member loyalty program, whose members drive more than a third of Cracker Barrel's revenue, the analyst says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Citigroup taps Adam Inzirillo from Cboe for equities business
data.description:
Citigroup > has hired Adam Inzirillo from Cboe Global Markets to lead its cash and futures execution platform within the bank’s equities division, according to a person with knowledge of the matter.

In his new role, Inzirillo will oversee Citi’s electronic execution platform, focusing on trading algorithms and direct market access offerings, the person said.

He will join in October as global head of the execution platform for the equities business and report to Sebastien Mailleux, global co-head of prime services, the person added.

Inzirillo had joined Cboe in 2019 as head of U.S. equities and was promoted to global head of data and access solutions in 2023 during a senior leadership reshuffle.

Citi's equity markets revenue rose 6% to $1.6 billion in the second quarter from a year earlier, boosted by momentum in its prime services business, which caters to hedge funds.

The prime business posted record balances in the second quarter, up nearly 27% from a year earlier.

Bloomberg News reported the move earlier on Friday.

data.title: Amer Sports Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
data.description: (17:45 GMT) Amer Sports Price Target Raised to $50.00/Share From $42.00 by Citigroup

data.title: INTC: Intel Stock Falls After Top Analyst Calls Nvidia Deal Overhyped
data.description:
Sep 19 - Intel Corporation came under pressure Friday after Citi cut its rating to Sell following the company's recently announced deal with Nvidia . Shares of Intel slipped more than 3% in morning trading.

Citi analyst Christopher Danely argued the Nvidia partnership is unlikely to fix Intel's core challenges. He noted that better graphics will not improve Intel's CPUs enough to outpace rivals like AMD, where the processor remains the key performance driver.

The analyst also downplayed expectations for Intel's AI products, estimating the total addressable market at just $1 billion to $2 billion, far smaller than what investors may be pricing in.

Danely lowered his stance on Intel to Sell from Neutral, but simultaneously lifted the price target to $29 from $24. He suggested the stock's sharp rally, up about 50% since early August, reflects investor hopes for Intel's foundry unit. Yet Citi sees minimal chance of success, given Taiwan Semiconductor remains years ahead.

The downgrade highlights the market's skepticism over Intel's turnaround, even as Nvidia continues to set the pace in AI chips.

data.title: Dexcom falls to four-month low
data.description:
** Shares of medical device maker DexCom fall 9.7% to over four-month low of $68.47

** Hunterbrook Capital said on Thursday that it is short on DXCM, citing "inaccurate readings" from co's G7 continuous glucose monitor; shares of DXCM closed down about 1% on Thursday

** Reuters could not independently verify the claims

** The short report adds to concerns "emphasizing G7 reliability allegations," TD Cowen analysts said in a note

** Still, we acknowledge that uncertainty around co's G7 device "will continue to weigh on the stock, likely until we see third-quarter results indicative of continued momentum"- William Blair analysts

** Including session moves, stock down 12.2% YTD

data.title: PLTR: Salesforce CEO Slams Palantir's Sky-High Valuation, Says 'Most Expensive Ever'
data.description:
Sep 19 - Palantir Technologies continues to draw Wall Street's attention, with Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff weighing in on its soaring valuation.

Speaking at Goldman Sachs' Communacopia+Technology conference, Benioff said he is inspired by Palantir's growth but joked the company trades at 100 times revenue, maybe 1000 times soon.

The comment underscores the market disconnect between the two software firms. Palantir, with 48% revenue growth, carries a $406 billion market cap, while Salesforce, despite generating more than $10 billion in quarterly revenue, stands at $231 billion and has seen its shares fall 27% this year.

Benioff also criticized Palantir's high prices, calling them the most expensive enterprise software he has ever seen.

The contrast reflects a broader debate: whether investors should prioritize hypergrowth and premium pricing, or scale and established income streams. The rivalry is intensifying, as Salesforce recently secured a U.S. Army contract over Palantir.

data.title: Lower Mortgage Rates Not Showing Up In Home Sales, Lennar Co-CEO Says — Market Talk
data.description: Mortgage rates are steadily declining, falling this week to their lowest level since 2024. But that yet hasn't led to a boost in sales for Lennar, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "Even though mortgage rates began to trend downward towards the end of the quarter, stronger sales have not yet followed," Miller says. But the company is starting to see stronger customer interest and traffic and expects sales to eventually pick up if mortgage rates continue to fall. "We are optimistic that if mortgage rates approach the 6% level or even lower, we will soon see some firming in the market," Miller says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Paul Costelloe nods to 1960s, broken dolls inspire Bora Aksu at London Fashion Week
data.description:
Irish designer Paul Costelloe took fashionistas back to 1960s California while Turkish-born Bora Aksu celebrated cracks and imperfections at London Fashion Week on Friday.

Setting the scene on Rodeo Drive in 1967, Costelloe opened his "Boulevard of Dreams" spring-summer 2026 presentation with short feminine creations in pale pink, yellow and blue. There were jackets with pointy collars or bows, embellished minis and shift dresses. All were paired with matching platform shoes.

Models wore floral and frilly designs that nodded to 1960s fashion, including plenty of short dresses as well as cut-out gowns.

“It’s a very happy collection. It very much reflects California in the late sixties,” Costelloe told Reuters. “The inspiration has been from the ‘Valley of the Dolls’... It’s very much West Coast of America and it's very chic, very fresh, very exciting."

Aksu said that this season he turned to his own collection of broken dolls for inspiration.

Models wore dresses embellished with layers, embroidery and plenty of lace trimmings.

Aksu put frills on sleeves, large shiny sequins on skirts and see-through gloves and intricate florals on frocks.

The looks were layered: tiered dresses or jackets over long blouses that hung over skirts. Models also wore bonnet hats tied under the neck and adorned with bows or sequins.

"I feel like we are like the dolls... we have... our hearts broken or we go through things. But... we still kind of survive and then it becomes part of us," Aksu told Reuters.

"With the dolls, with all these cracks and defects, I was thinking, I want to keep this and I bring it to... life again. So it's not about covering their cracks but it’s about embracing them."

London Fashion Week, which kicked off on Thursday evening and runs until Monday, is the second leg of the spring-summer 2026 catwalk calendar, which began in New York and then heads to Milan and Paris.

On the programme are 157 designers and organisations, including 50 catwalk shows and a mix of emerging as well as established designers like Erdem, Roksanda and fashion giant Burberry.

data.title: Jefferies' DeSanctis sees another 8% gain for the Russell 2000 this year
data.description:
Key points:
  • Major U.S. stock indexes rise; Nasdaq out front up 0.5%
  • Tech leads S&P sector gainers; energy off most
  • Dollar, gold rise; crude falls >1%, bitcoin off ~1.5%
  • U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield inches up to ~4.14%

JEFFERIES' DESANCTIS SEES ANOTHER 8% GAIN FOR THE RUSSELL 2000 THIS YEAR

After the Russell 2000 marked a record closing high on Thursday, Jefferies equity strategist Steven DeSanctis raised his target for the small cap index and started his research note with the words "Don't fight the Fed."

DeSanctis said in a research note issued late on Thursday that he now expects the RUT to hit 2665 by year-end, implying an 8% increase from Thursday's close, powered by above consensus earnings growth of 5.5% for 2025. DeSanctis said the prior 4% target for earnings growth seemed too low even though it was already head of the Street forecast for 3.3% growth, he said.

Prior to Thursday's move, Jefferies had started the year with a 2715 year-end target, which it dropped to down to 2200 after Liberation Day, when the market went into crisis mode after U.S. President Donald Trump first announced hefty import tariffs.

DeSanctis noted that when the RUT broke a long dry spell on Thursday after having had gone 967 days without a new high. Its last closing high was in November 2021.

The small cap index was boosted by the Federal Reserve's Wednesday announcement that it was cutting interest rates and its indication that more cuts are coming. Smaller companies are highly sensitive to interest rate changes since they depend so much on borrowing.

In the wake of the "Liberation Day" meltdown, DeSanctis had moved to favor domestic stocks - with <20% of revenue coming from outside the U.S. - rather than foreign stocks.

But since the market low, he notes that domestic names have gained 30.5% vs. 53.6% for names with high foreign sales. So he said: "We take the mulligan, moving back to names with more overseas exposure."

And the strategist also points out that those stocks tend to outperform after Fed cuts, and beat domestic stocks when GDP is >2%, with Jefferies' target for 2026 at 2.2%.

However DeSanctis said: "The one knock is Domestic is cheaper."

On Friday the RUT shied away from its record a little and was last down 0.6%.

(Sinéad Carew)

*****

data.title: Homebuilders Need To Build More Affordably, Lennar Co-CEO Says — Market Talk
data.description: The sluggish housing market and affordability concerns from buyers have forced Lennar to build homes more cheaply, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says, adding that the whole industry will need to continue that push. "We're going to have to find, frankly, as an industry, a way to build and deliver homes at a more affordable level," Miller says, mentioning potential cost reductions in land financing and construction. He also says Lennar is building new technologies to cut costs that are expensive to do. "The estimate goes up before it goes down," he says. "We have to get more efficient and effective." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Insurgents raid Nigerian military barracks in Banki, soldiers retreat, residents and sources say
data.description:
By Ahmed Kingimi

Suspected insurgents attacked a border town in northeastern Nigeria's Borno state and seized weapons from a military barracks as the soldiers there fled, residents and security sources said on Friday.

The incident occurred in the town of Banki in Bama district.

Insurgents in the area have upped their attacks this year against civilians and security forces.

An internal security memo from the Civilian Joint Task Force, a paramilitary unit that helps the military tackle Islamist fighters, showed the attack started at about 2130 GMT on Thursday and ended in the early hours of Friday.

"The insurgents overran the town and entered the military barracks, seizing ammunition and weapons. The commanding officer and soldiers reportedly abandoned their positions, fleeing towards Cameroon and leaving civilians behind," read the memo seen by Reuters.

One soldier from 152 Battalion, stationed in the town, said the insurgents came in large numbers, forcing security forces to retreat after heavy gunfire.

The Nigerian Army did not respond to a request for comment.

Boko Haram fighters occupied Banki a decade ago and the town is one of a number in northeast Nigeria that has repeatedly been attacked by the group as well as by Islamic State West Africa Province fighters, leading to a humanitarian crisis there.

Aliyu Haruna, a Banki resident, said on Friday he had seen at least seven dead bodies in the town, including those of three soldiers. Reuters could not independently confirm the number of fatalities.

"Both military and paramilitary personnel fled to Cameroon, and it was only this morning that they returned," Haruna told Reuters by phone.

data.title: Russia says it presumes Trump still pursuing Ukraine peace efforts
data.description:
The Kremlin said on Friday it presumed that U.S. President Donald Trump was still pursuing efforts to achieve a settlement to the Ukraine conflict despite Trump's statement that Russian President Vladimir Putin had let him down through his actions.

Trump has expressed disappointment several times at Moscow's continued attacks on Ukrainian targets despite his efforts to broker a solution. On Thursday, he told a joint news conference alongside British Prime Minister Keir Starmer that Putin "has let me down."

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov, quoted by Russian state news agency RIA, said Trump's reaction was understandable.

"We presume that the United States, and President Trump personally, have maintained the political will and the intention to expend efforts to facilitate a Ukrainian settlement," RIA quoted Peskov as saying. "So, of course, President Trump is, shall we say, quite emotional about this topic. This is completely understandable."

Putin and other Russian officials have praised Trump's efforts to put together a solution to the Ukrainian conflict and organise top-level meetings.

data.title: Soybeans Slide on Trade Uncertainty, Global Supply
data.description:
Soybean futures fell toward $10.25 per bushel, pressured by lingering uncertainty over US–China trade talks.

Traders had anticipated that the meeting between President Trump and President Xi might trigger new Chinese purchases, but the lack of concrete developments led to earlier-week declines.

The market had been hopeful for a major sale or a framework agreement, yet nothing materialized.

Large global supplies and weak Chinese demand continue to weigh on prices, even as harvest pressures mount.

Analysts caution that while short-term support may surface, ongoing US harvest progress and expectations for a bumper global corn crop could push soybeans lower.

data.title: Microsoft's stock has been in a rut since earnings. What's going on?
data.description:
By Emily Bary

Analysts offer a few theories for the seemingly 'unexplainable' way the tech giant's stock has fallen out of favor in recent weeks

Microsoft has fallen out of favor with Big Tech investors in recent weeks.

Microsoft Corp. had arguably the strongest earnings report within Big Tech last quarter, at least relative to investor expectations. Yet its stock is flat since just before the results came out, as nearly every other member of the "Magnificent Seven" has moved higher.

Executives at Microsoft (MSFT) "sound fantastic in buy-side meetings as of late," according to Mizuho desk-based analyst Jordan Klein, and the company is levered to OpenAI's booming demand as well as upbeat cloud trends even outside of artificial intelligence. Microsoft has also told investors what to expect on margins for the next 12 months through guidance that was "good" and added clarity, Klein noted.

So why don't investors seem very interested in the stock?

It's a dynamic that's "unexplainable," Klein wrote on Friday, noting he's gotten "zero good answers" when he's posed the question lately in nearly all of his investor conversations.

But whether there are no answers or just no good answers may be more of the issue. Klein does have a few theories around why investors may be dodging Microsoft's stock lately. For one, they may be worried about how Microsoft's Azure cloud-computing growth will fare once OpenAI "jumps ship and takes their act on road solo." OpenAI reportedly has a $300 billion cloud deal with Oracle Corp. (ORCL) over the coming years that "implies they want off ... Azure sooner vs. later," according to Klein.

Read: Why the $300 billion Oracle-OpenAI deal could be fueling an AI bubble

There's also the fact that Microsoft is a software company, and Klein has seen some investors rotate out of the software sector in favor of semiconductors.

Evercore ISI analyst Kirk Materne explored the phenomenon as well in a note to clients last week. At the time of his Sept. 9 report, he said he was starting to get more investor questions about Microsoft's lagging postearnings stock performance relative to the S&P 500 SPX.

"On a fundamental basis, we don't see much to justify the recent underperformance," he wrote. "That said, the new 'marketecture' around the 'Mag 7' means that [Microsoft] will, at times, lose some momentum if another 'Mag 7' member is seeing a material shift in sentiment" - which had been happening as Apple Inc.'s stock (AAPL) began to see new shine in the eyes of investors.

Materne noted there was a high bar for Microsoft's Azure cloud-computing unit after the company stunned Wall Street with 39% growth last quarter, "but the trends underpinning that growth - namely ChatGPT inferencing and solid enterprise demand - seem stable."

Don't miss: Why Microsoft's 'must-own' stock looks even more like a no-brainer after earnings

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
